content.problemStatement:
One evening Johnny found some funny looking beens in his grandfather's garden shed, and decided to plant one of them. Next morning, to his surprise he found an enormous beanstalk growing in his back yard. Undaunted by its size, he decided to count its leaves.


You must know that beanstalks in Byteland grow in a very special way. At the lowest (1st) level, there is exactly one stem. At any level(including the 1st), a stem can end (forming exactly one leaf), or branch into exactly two stems which grow into the next level, following the same rules.


Johnny believes he has managed to count the number of leaves at each of the levels of the beanstalk. However, you must know that before he began to count, Johnny ate one or two of the other beans he found in his grandfather's shed, and that's why he is not quite sure of his results. Please verify whether Johnny's results may possibly be correct, at least in theory.
constraints: Sum of k over all test cases ≤ 2·10⁷
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer t (1 ≤ t ≤ 20), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer k (1 ≤ k ≤ 10⁶) – the number of levels. The next line contains k space‑separated non‑negative integers l₁ … l_k (0 ≤ l_i ≤ 10⁶) – the reported number of leaves at each level, starting from level 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing either "Yes" if a beanstalk that follows the Bytelandian growth rules can have exactly the given leaf counts, or "No" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: start with 1 stem. Level 1 leaf=0 → stems become 2. Level 2 leaf=1 (≤2) → stems become 2·(2‑1)=2. Level 3 leaf=2 (≤2) → stems become 0. No rule is violated → Yes.\nTest case 2: after level 2 we have 2 stems, but leaf count at level 3 is 3 > 2, impossible → No.
All leaf counts are zero, so each level simply doubles the number of stems. No constraint is broken.
Level 1: leaf=1 ≤ 1, stems become 0. Level 2: leaf=1 > 0, impossible.

solution.approach: We simulate the growth level by level. Let s be the number of stems that reach the current level (initially s = 1). For each reported leaf count l_i we check: if l_i > s the report is impossible → answer "No". Otherwise the stems that do not become leaves branch into two new stems, so the number of stems for the next level becomes s = 2·(s – l_i). The value can grow very large, therefore we cap it at a safe constant (4·10¹⁸) that fits in 64‑bit unsigned integer; capping does not affect correctness because any value above the cap is already far larger than any possible leaf count (≤10⁶). After processing all k levels without violation we answer "Yes". The algorithm is O(k) per test case and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total number of levels) = O(∑k) ≤ O(2·10⁷)
